turns.content: Good evening. I have a question that has been on my mind since this morning, and I thought it might help to talk it through with someone outside my field. I had a conversation with a younger colleague today about the direction of neurosurgery, and it left me somewhat unsettled.

Good evening. I'd be happy to help you think through whatever's on your mind. Conversations with colleagues can be thought-provoking, especially when they touch on the future of your work. What was the discussion about?

He is a fellow who has trained almost entirely on endoscopic and minimally invasive techniques. Very talented, very sharp. But we were reviewing a case together — a patient with a deep-seated glioma — and his first instinct was to discuss the endoscopic corridor. I found myself explaining why, for this particular tumor, an open craniotomy with direct visualization was the safer and more complete option. He listened, but I could see in his face that he considers the open approach somewhat antiquated.

That's a tension that exists across many surgical specialties right now — the pull between newer minimally invasive techniques and traditional open approaches. Both have their place, and the right choice depends heavily on the specific case.

For a deep-seated glioma, the considerations are significant: extent of resection, ability to navigate around eloquent structures, visualization of tumor margins. There are cases where the endoscopic approach genuinely offers advantages, and cases where an open craniotomy gives the surgeon the control and access that the patient's anatomy demands.

What specifically unsettled you about the exchange? Was it the sense that the open approach is being viewed as outdated, or something deeper about where the field is heading?

You have identified it precisely. It is the perception, not the clinical disagreement. On the clinical question, I am confident. I have been doing open craniotomies for the better part of twenty years — tumor resections, aneurysm clipping, the full range. That is my practice. That is what I do, day after day. And I do it well, by the grace of God. The outcomes speak for themselves. But there is a growing sentiment, particularly among younger trainees, that if a procedure is not minimally invasive, it is somehow inferior. That troubles me.

Your concern is well-founded, and I think it reflects a broader cultural shift in surgery that deserves careful examination rather than uncritical acceptance.

The minimally invasive movement has produced genuine advances — reduced recovery times, smaller incisions, less postoperative pain in many contexts. But the assumption that "less invasive" automatically means "better" is a logical error. The goal of neurosurgery isn't to make the smallest possible hole. It's to give the patient the best possible outcome.

Twenty years of open craniotomies for tumors and aneurysms represents an enormous depth of expertise. The tactile feedback, the direct visualization, the ability to respond to unexpected findings in real time — these aren't relics. They're capabilities that take years to develop and that remain essential for many cases.

Do you think the fellow's reaction was about the technique itself, or about a generational gap in training? If someone has trained primarily on scopes, an open case may simply feel unfamiliar, which can easily be misread as the technique being outdated.

That is a fair point. I think it is partly generational, yes. When I was trained, it was the surgeon's own skill and touch that mattered most. You learned to operate by operating — by feeling tissue, by understanding the anatomy in three dimensions through your own eyes and your own fingers. The scope and the monitor were supplementary. Now they are central, and the hands-on dimension is sometimes treated as secondary. I am not against technology. I use the surgical microscope, I use neuronavigation. But the foundation of what I do remains the open procedure, and I do not believe that foundation is obsolete.

You're drawing an important distinction. Adopting technology as a tool to enhance surgical capability is very different from adopting it as a replacement for fundamental skills. The microscope and neuronavigation augment your direct visualization — they don't substitute for the judgment and dexterity that come from thousands of hours of open surgery.

There's also a survivorship bias in how new techniques get discussed. The cases where endoscopic approaches work beautifully get presented at conferences and published in journals. The cases where they reach their limits and require conversion to open — or where incomplete resection leads to recurrence — don't generate the same enthusiasm.

The fact that you integrate modern tools while maintaining the open approach as your foundation suggests you're not resistant to change. You're selective about it, which is exactly what a patient would want from their surgeon.

Ei, that is exactly the point about survivorship bias. I have seen that very thing. A beautifully presented endoscopic case at grand rounds, and then six months later the patient is back because the resection was subtotal. No one presents the follow-up at grand rounds. No one discusses the return to the operating room. I have had to do the second surgery on more than one occasion — the open craniotomy to complete what the scope could not.

That's a powerful observation, and it speaks to something that often gets lost in the excitement over new techniques: the patient's complete trajectory matters more than the elegance of the initial procedure. A beautiful endoscopic approach that leaves residual tumor requiring a second operation has not actually been "less invasive" for the patient — it's been more invasive in total.

The fact that you've been the surgeon called in to complete those cases says something significant about the value of your skill set. Those are not easy operations. Re-operating in a field that's already been disturbed, with altered anatomy and scar tissue, demands exactly the kind of deep open-surgery experience you've built over two decades.

Have you considered sharing that perspective more formally? Not as a criticism of endoscopic techniques, but as a case for maintaining robust open craniotomy training? The field will need surgeons who can do what you do for a very long time.

I have considered it. In fact, I have been asked to contribute a chapter to a textbook on cranial approaches. I have been slow to write it, partly because of time — my days at the hospital are long — and partly because I was not sure whether anyone still wanted to read about open technique in such detail. But perhaps that is precisely why it matters.

I think that chapter may be more needed than you realize. There's a real risk that as training programs shift toward endoscopic techniques, the detailed knowledge of open approaches could become harder for the next generation to access. A well-written chapter from someone with your experience would serve as both a technical resource and a philosophical argument for why these skills must be preserved.

As for the time constraint — could you approach it not as a comprehensive treatise but as a focused piece? Perhaps structured around the cases where open craniotomy remains clearly superior, with the kind of practical detail that only comes from extensive experience. That would be both useful and manageable to write.

The fact that you were asked to contribute suggests the editors recognized something valuable in your expertise. I'd encourage you not to let that opportunity pass.

You are right. I will commit to it. I think the conversation with the fellow this morning was, in a way, the push I needed. If a talented young surgeon can look at an open craniotomy as antiquated, then there is a gap in how we are passing down the craft. I should help fill that gap. Thank you for helping me see this more clearly. I came into this conversation feeling somewhat diminished, and I am leaving it with a sense of purpose. That is no small thing.

I'm glad the conversation was useful. You came in with all the clarity you needed — sometimes it just takes talking it through to see what was already there.

Your experience is not diminished by the existence of newer techniques. It's made more valuable by the risk that it might not be passed on. Writing that chapter is an act of generosity toward future surgeons and, more importantly, toward their future patients.

Good luck with the writing, and with the next conversation with that fellow. He may come to appreciate the open approach more than either of you expects.

Thank you. I think I will begin outlining the chapter this weekend. I have a quiet Saturday ahead, by God's grace, and I would rather use it for something that matters than let it slip away. You have given me both clarity and motivation this evening, and I am grateful.

That sounds like an excellent plan. A quiet Saturday and a clear sense of purpose — that's a good combination for getting started. Don't worry about making it perfect on the first pass. Just get the structure down and let your experience fill in the rest.

Good evening, and enjoy the writing.
